content:
Would it be worth adding a macro like this to utils?

/// If the first expression is true, return `Some(second_expression)`. Otherwise
/// return `None`.
///
/// ```
/// let t = true;
/// let f = false;
/// assert_eq!(if_then_some(t, "hello"), Some("hello"));
/// assert_eq!(if_then_some(f, "hello"), None);
/// ```
///
/// This is a version of `bool::then`that can be used in const contexts
macro_rules! if_then_some {
    ($if_this:expr, $then_some:expr) => {
        if $if_this {
            Some($then_some)
        } else {
            None
        }
    };
}

Just to turn this sort of registration struct pattern (from @FUJITA Tomonori's patch) into easier one liners

+    Opaque::new(bindings::phy_driver {
// ...
+        soft_reset: if T::HAS_SOFT_RESET {
+            Some(Adapter::<T>::soft_reset_callback)
+        } else {
+            None
+        },
+        get_features: if T::HAS_GET_FEATURES {
+            Some(Adapter::<T>::get_features_callback)
+        } else {
+            None
+        },
+        match_phy_device: if T::HAS_MATCH_PHY_DEVICE {
+            Some(Adapter::<T>::match_phy_device_callback)
+        } else {
+            None
+        },
+        suspend: if T::HAS_SUSPEND {
+            Some(Adapter::<T>::suspend_callback)
+        } else {
+            None
+        },

 Opaque::new(bindings::phy_driver {
    soft_reset: if_then_else(T::HAS_SOFT_RESET, Adapter::<T>::soft_reset_callback),
    get_features:  if_then_else(T::HAS_GET_FEATURES , Adapter::<T>::get_features_callback),
    match_phy_device: if_then_else(T::HAS_MATCH_PHY_DEVICE, Adapter::<T>::match_phy_device_callback),
    // ...
}
There is bool::then_some but it isn't const (and won't be for a while because it needs const drop) so you can't use it to help with the static initialization structs
Looks handy. More specific to vtable initialization might even make sense because we know the patterns; e.g., soft_reset method uses HAS_SOFT_RESET condition and soft_reset_callback.

I was trying to come up with something that #[vtable] would be able to add to make this easier since it's such a common pattern, but I can't come up with anything. It's tricky since most of the time we need something const so we can use statics, otherwise something like an autogenerated const builder pattern would work

In general, we try to avoid macros as much as possible, but when it makes sense, we can add them, of course.

In this case, even with the macro, they couldn't be one-liners, no? The match_phy_device line would be 107 characters.
I think it's fine to just keep it as-is. Sure, it's a bit verbose, but nobody is going to be confused about what the code is doing.

I think that if we want to have a macro, then it should be even shorter, something like

make_vtable!(bindings::phy_driver {
    soft_reset,
    get_features,
    match_phy_devices,
    // ...
});

General comment regarding vtables: I don't like that the default implementation a given trait doesn't get called and it's not straightforward to notice this: HAS_SOMETHING evaluates to true only if the user implements the SOMETHING function, and not when SOMETHING is a default implementation for the trait. The above macro would make it even more difficult to realize that the default implementation is never called.

not exactly sure what your problem is, when HAS_FOO is false, we store None into the vtable. the C side will then see the null pointer and use the C default implementation. if you now have a Rust function that takes an impl Foo, we can only call the trait function and if the user has not implemented one, we have to have some default.
My problem is that the default trait implementation for that function is never used.

e.g. in the file operations

pub trait Operations {
...
    /// Changes the position of the file.
    ///
    /// Corresponds to the `llseek` function pointer in `struct file_operations`.
    fn seek(
        _data: <Self::Data as ForeignOwnable>::Borrowed<'_>,
        _file: &File,
        _offset: SeekFrom,
    ) -> Result<u64> {
        Err(EINVAL)
    }

The default implementation returns EINVAL, but that's never actually called:

        llseek: if T::HAS_SEEK {
            Some(Self::llseek_callback)
        } else {
            None
        },

because if the user doesn't implement seek themselves, then a null pointer will be stored in the vtable.

it will possibly be used later when we write rust code that builds on top of operations:

pub fn some_special_rust_file_operation<O: Operations>(
    data: <O::Data as ForeignOwnable>::Borrowed<'_>, file: &File
) -> Result<()> {
    let offset = ...;
    let pos = O::seek(data, file, offset)?;
    // ...
    Ok(())
}
T::HAS_SEEK returns true only when the user implements their own version of seek. Because of this, the default implementation for seek can never be called.
can never be called by the C callback I mean
yes, but I dont see the problem.
it will only ever be called by rust code

What do you expect to happen when you read:

 fn seek(
        _data: <Self::Data as ForeignOwnable>::Borrowed<'_>,
        _file: &File,
        _offset: SeekFrom,
    ) -> Result<u64> {
        Err(EINVAL)
    }

?
well, I see a default implementation of a trait function. the implementers of the trait can override that if needed
Yes, but this default implementation is not called. Because if the user doesn't override it, than the vtable will contain NULL instead of a pointer to this function.
I'm sorry, I still do not understand why that is a problem.
Because it's confusing.
what would be the alternative?
Don't provide any default implementation. If the user doesn't implement the function, leave it NULL in the vtable. Of course, this is not supported by the Rust syntax for traits.
Or some way to mark a function in the trait optional without providing a default implementation.
but what are we then supposed to do when someone calls the function on the rust side? (it wont even compile, since every function needs to be implemented)
Who would call it from Rust besides the llseek_callback?
e.g. if someone decides to add certain helper functions for common filesystem operations?
Well, the problem is that the C code already implements default operations (in some cases) in case the function pointer is NULL and the driver doesn't implement the operation.
I think we should then actually just call that default C function in the default impl of the rust function

If we could do this, it would actually be great, because it would be clear what is happening. Unfortunately, I think a lot of this behavior is driven by if (!op->something) { do_some_default_thing();} on the C side.
but then just call do_some_default_thing()? or would it often just be return EINVAL;?
Yeah, in this case it would be enough to call do_some_default_thing(). I didn't look in detail to see if this would be feasible in the general case or not.
But I think the underlying issue is that NULL is interpreted by the C side in two different ways: either the functionality is missing or a default implementation is used.

A few handlers on the C side behave differently depending on whether the pointer is null. If it’s non-null, it obviously calls the function, otherwise it has some non-trivial behaviour that we don’t want to replicate on the Rust side because it’s also subject to change on the C side.

And we have a default implementation in Rust because that’s the only way to make a trait function optional.

I agree it may be confusing, perhaps we should add a comment as you suggested at dinner, Ariel, but I think that’s the best solution we have.

If you have a better idea, we’d love to use it.

Yeah, this is the best solution for now. A comment would be good and, if I remember correctly, you also mentioned we should call panic from the default implementation, that way it's even clearer that the function is not supposed to be called.
I disagree, we should not call panic!, since the kernel should never panic.
rather return an error like Err(EINVAL) or do nothing.

Depends on the actually function if it can return an Error. But calling panic should be wrong. It would make sense if its a build time panic, but not sure if that would be possible to implement correctly
debug_assert! (or similar approaches) + return an error could be another alternative that may be a good balance.

Ariel Miculas said:

General comment regarding vtables: I don't like that the default implementation a given trait doesn't get called and it's not straightforward to notice this: HAS_SOMETHING evaluates to true only if the user implements the SOMETHING function, and not when SOMETHING is a default implementation for the trait. The above macro would make it even more difficult to realize that the default implementation is never called.

Default trait should just be unreachable!(), then it is easy to infer for the reader.

Benno Lossin said:

e.g. if someone decides to add certain helper functions for common filesystem operations?

The entry point should be the C side vtable

Benno Lossin said:

I disagree, we should not call panic!, since the kernel should never panic.

It is a bug if this code is called, so firing off a BUG() seems reasonable.
why would it be a bug if the default implementation is called? I still think we want to support the use case of someone writing subsystem code that uses the driver traits.
Because it's supposed to be a callback. The entry point in the callback is in the generic subsystem code, on the C side. I don't see any reason why you would want to call it from other places.
And the default implementation is just a hack, so you can omit implementing that particular function. But it's never called, the pointer is just set to NULL in the vtable.
I am thinking of the future use case where someone wants to write subsystemcode in rust.
Then you can modify the default implementation, right?
but then we have a weird mix of functions that should never be called and functions that you are allowed to call. why not just implement the default behavior for all?
Wedson already answered this question.

Plus I think it's better to see unimplemented instead of throwing some error that will never be thrown. Going back to my seek example, you can see it returns EINVAL. But the actual error returned from the VFS layer is ESPIPE. So one may wonder why EINVAL is not returned if one never overrides the default implementation for seek.

Ariel Miculas said:

Wedson already answered this question.

I do not know how the C side looks like, but maybe we could just call the C function that provides the default behavior.

Ariel Miculas said:

Plus I think it's better to see unimplemented instead of throwing some error that will never be thrown. Going back to my seek example, you can see it returns EINVAL. But the actual error returned from the VFS layer is ESPIPE. So one may wonder why EINVAL is not returned if one never overrides the default implementation for seek.

I think that we then should not even have a default body for the function. instead add a #[optional] attribute to the function . this attribute tells the #[vtable] macro that the function is optional and it then adds a body with either a build_unreachable!("<explanation of why this function must not be called>") or a normal unreachable! (but I would not really like that).
You also need a way to tell the trait that the function is optional, so the user doesn't have to implement it. If it can be done in a different way, then great!

what I meant by the #[optional] attribute:

// mod fs:
#[vtable]
pub trait Operations {
    #[optional]
    fn seek(...);
}
I like it.
I'll see if our build_error function is sufficient and if it is, I will make a patch

Benno Lossin said:

Ariel Miculas said:

Wedson already answered this question.

I do not know how the C side looks like, but maybe we could just call the C function that provides the default behavior.

I wish it were that simple. I'll show you a couple of examples.

Consider do_dentry_open: https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/fs/open.c#L919 -- here it modifies flags based on the function pointers being non-null. We can't really replicate this by "calling the C function that provides the default behaviour". With some luck we could potentially find a place where we could replicate this behaviour, but even this wouldn't be ideal because we would have to keep it in sync with the C side.

Let's look at __kernel_read (https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/fs/read_write.c#L418): 

    /*
     * Also fail if ->read_iter and ->read are both wired up as that
     * implies very convoluted semantics.
     */
    if (unlikely(!file->f_op->read_iter || file->f_op->read))
        return warn_unsupported(file, "read");

vfs_read, however, allows both of them to coexist (https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/fs/read_write.c#L467) and uses a static (new_sync_read) function to emulate read from read_iter. do_iter_read does something similar but the other way around, it uses a static (do_loop_readv_writev) function to emulate read_iter from read. Let's say we convince that C maintainers to expose those functions to us so that we can have default implementations. If a module were to leave both of them unimplemented (i.e., using the default implementation), we would have an infinite recursion.

file_needs_f_pos_lock checks iterate_shared (https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/fs/file.c#L1052):

static inline bool file_needs_f_pos_lock(struct file *file)
{
        return (file->f_mode & FMODE_ATOMIC_POS) &&
                (file_count(file) > 1 || file->f_op->iterate_shared);
}

Benno Lossin said:

what I meant by the #[optional] attribute:

// mod fs:
#[vtable]
pub trait Operations {
    #[optional]
    fn seek(...);
}

Let's be mindful not to over use macros. It does look simpler, but we have no hint as to what it does. If we had simply:

#[vtable]
pub trait Operations {
    fn seek(...) {
        unreachable!()
    }
}

Then we know it's optional and is unreachable.
I would prefer kernel::bulid_error("...")
Sure, I meant that an inline impl should be considered vs the optional attribute being discussed.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Benno Lossin said:

Ariel Miculas said:

Wedson already answered this question.

I do not know how the C side looks like, but maybe we could just call the C function that provides the default behavior.

I wish it were that simple. I'll show you a couple of examples.

Consider do_dentry_open: https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/fs/open.c#L919 -- here it modifies flags based on the function pointers being non-null. We can't really replicate this by "calling the C function that provides the default behaviour". With some luck we could potentially find a place where we could replicate this behaviour, but even this wouldn't be ideal because we would have to keep it in sync with the C side.

Let's look at __kernel_read (https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/fs/read_write.c#L418): 

    /*
     * Also fail if ->read_iter and ->read are both wired up as that
     * implies very convoluted semantics.
     */
    if (unlikely(!file->f_op->read_iter || file->f_op->read))
        return warn_unsupported(file, "read");

vfs_read, however, allows both of them to coexist (https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/fs/read_write.c#L467) and uses a static (new_sync_read) function to emulate read from read_iter. do_iter_read does something similar but the other way around, it uses a static (do_loop_readv_writev) function to emulate read_iter from read. Let's say we convince that C maintainers to expose those functions to us so that we can have default implementations. If a module were to leave both of them unimplemented (i.e., using the default implementation), we would have an infinite recursion.

file_needs_f_pos_lock checks iterate_shared (https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/fs/file.c#L1052):

static inline bool file_needs_f_pos_lock(struct file *file)
{
        return (file->f_mode & FMODE_ATOMIC_POS) &&
                (file_count(file) > 1 || file->f_op->iterate_shared);
}

I see, I did not know that it was this intertwined. In that case I think having a build error is fine.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Sure, I meant that an inline impl should be considered vs the optional attribute being discussed.

I still think that the macro is actually better:

  • we do not need to prefix the parameters with _,
  • writing #[vtable] traits is simpler, because you do not have to think about what to put in the body of the functions,
  • readers also probably know much faster what it means vs reading the comment/error message of the build_error call.

I could miss the detail this time, but last time the question was brought up, I suggested we can do:

https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/291565-Help/topic/C.20callbacks.20in.20vtables/near/380416460
